Assignment 3

Jordi Florit Ensenyat, Josep Gabriel Fornés Reynés, Pau Girón Rodríguez

2024-11-25

1. Introduction

This assignment aims to build predictive models to classify the loan status variable (approved/not approved) for potential applicants using the loan_data.csv dataset. The dataset, available on Kaggle, is a synthetic version inspired by the original Credit Risk dataset and enriched with additional variables based on Financial Risk for Loan Approval data. The analysis will be structured as follows:

  • Data Analysis: Analyze key features, distribution patterns, relationships, plots, missing data, and outliers to better understand credit risk factors.

  • Data Processing: Prepare the data by handling missing values, outliers, and normalizing features to ensure the dataset is ready for modeling.

  • Classification Methods: Evaluate different classification methods for modeling loan approval, using various structures and parameters, and compare the models obtained.

This structured approach ensures a comprehensive analysis and comparison of different models to identify the most effective one for predicting loan approval.

1.1. Dataset Information

The dataset, available on Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/taweilo/loan-approval-classification-data) is a synthetic version inspired by the original Credit Risk dataset and enriched with additional variables based on Financial Risk for Loan Approval data. SMOTENC was used to simulate new data points to enlarge the instances. The dataset is structured for both categorical and continuous features.

1.2. Variable Information

The dataset contains 45,000 records and 14 variables. Below is a detailed description of each variable:

1. person age: Age of the person (numeric)

2. person gender: Gender of the person (categorical: female, male)

3. person education: Highest education level (categorical: Associate, Bachelor, Doctorate, High School, Master)

4. person income: Annual income (numeric)

5. person emp exp: Years of employment experience (integer)

6. person home ownership: Home ownership status (categorical: MORTGAGE, OTHER, OWN, RENT)

7. loan amnt: Loan amount requested (numeric)

8. loan intent: Purpose of the loan (categorical: DEBTCONSOLIDATION, EDUCATION, HOMEIMPROVEMENT, MEDICAL, PERSONAL, VENTURE)

9. loan int rate: Loan interest rate (numeric)

10. loan percent income: Loan amount as a percentage of annual income (numeric)

11. cb person cred hist length: Length of credit history in years (numeric)

12. credit score: Credit score of the person (integer)

13. previous loan defaults on file: Indicator of previous loan defaults (categorical: No, Yes)

Class label (target variable)

14. loan status: Loan approval status (integer 1 = approved; 0 = rejected)

1.3. Research Question

Utilize the data provided to build predictive models to classify the loan status variable (approved/not approved) for potential applicants. To answer this question you will:

a) (5 POINTS) Perform Explanatory Data Analysis: analyze key features, distribution patterns, relationships, plots, missing data, outliers, … to better understand credit risk factors.

b) (20 POINTS) Consider different classification methods for modelling loan approval. For each method, use different structures and/or parameters, different training/testing sets, compare the different models obtained and comment your results. Which model is the “best”?

2. Data Analisis

2.1. Importing Data

First of all, we have to import our data. Here, we are loading the necessary libraries and reading the CSV file containing the loan data.

# Load the necessary libraries and read the CSV file containing the loan data
library(tidyverse)
## Warning: package 'tidyverse' was built under R version 4.4.2
## Warning: package 'readr' was built under R version 4.4.2
## Warning: package 'lubridate' was built under R version 4.4.2

## ✔ lubridate 1.9.3     ✔ tidyr     1.3.1
## ✔ purrr     1.0.2

## ✖ dplyr::lag()    masks stats::lag()

library(ggcorrplot)

library(gridExtra)
## 
## Adjuntando el paquete: 'gridExtra'

library(dplyr)
library(e1071)

library(caret)

library(rpart.plot)

data <- read.table("loan_data.csv", header = TRUE, sep = ",")

This is one simple example of the dataset:

# Display the structure of the dataset
str(data)
## 'data.frame':    45000 obs. of  14 variables:
##  $ person_age                    : num  22 21 25 23 24 21 26 24 24 21 ...
##  $ person_gender                 : chr  "female" "female" "female" "female" ...
##  $ person_education              : chr  "Master" "High School" "High School" "Bachelor" ...

The summary data. The summary() function gives a statistical summary of each column, including minimum, maximum, mean, and quartiles.

2.2. Missing Values

# Identify missing values
colSums(is.na(data))
##                     person_age                  person_gender 
##                              0                              0 
##               person_education                  person_income 
##                              0                              0 
##                 person_emp_exp          person_home_ownership 
##                              0                              0 
##                      loan_amnt                    loan_intent 
##                              0                              0 
##                  loan_int_rate            loan_percent_income 
##                              0                              0 
##     cb_person_cred_hist_length                   credit_score 
##                              0                              0 
## previous_loan_defaults_on_file                    loan_status 
##                              0                              0

The function counts the number of missing values in each column of the dataframe. We can see that there are no missing values. This confirms that there are no NA values in the dataset, which is crucial to avoid errors in further analysis.

2.3. Outliers

As we can see into the summary, there are some unusual data (outliers):

· person_age: the maximum age of a person in this column is 144, which is not very credible and strange data. So now, we are going to show the possible outliers into the person_age data. Since we are talking in terms of requesting loans, we are going to assume that people over 80 years old are not going to request a loan. Now let’s visualize people over 80 years old.

# Filter rows directly to identify potential outliers in person_age
outliers_age <- data[data$person_age > 80, ]

# Display the first few rows
outliers_age
##       person_age person_gender person_education person_income person_emp_exp
## 82           144          male         Bachelor        300616            125
## 184          144          male        Associate        241424            121
## 576          123        female      High School         97140            101
## 748          123          male         Bachelor         94723            100
## 32298        144        female        Associate       7200766            124
## 32417         94          male      High School         29738             76
## 32507         84          male      High School        114705             61
## 37931        116          male         Bachelor       5545545             93
## 38114        109          male      High School       5556399             85
##       person_home_ownership loan_amnt loan_intent loan_int_rate
## 82                     RENT      4800     VENTURE         13.57
## 184                MORTGAGE      6000   EDUCATION         11.86
## 576                    RENT     20400   EDUCATION         10.25
## 748                    RENT     20000     VENTURE         11.01
## 32298              MORTGAGE      5000    PERSONAL         12.73
## 32417                  RENT      6500     MEDICAL         11.01
## 32507              MORTGAGE     10000    PERSONAL          7.51
## 37931              MORTGAGE      3823     VENTURE         12.15
## 38114              MORTGAGE      6195     VENTURE         12.58
##       loan_percent_income cb_person_cred_hist_length credit_score

## 576                  0.21                          3          805
## 748                  0.21                          4          714
## 32298                0.00                         25          850
## 32417                0.22                         27          773
## 32507                0.09                         24          784
## 37931                0.00                         24          708
## 38114                0.00                         22          792
##       previous_loan_defaults_on_file loan_status
## 82                                No           0
## 184                               No           0
## 576                              Yes           0
## 748                              Yes           0
## 32298                             No           0
## 32417                             No           0
## 32507                            Yes           0
## 37931                             No           0
## 38114                             No           0

· person_emp_exp: there is a person with 125 years of work experience, which makes us think that it is unusual and erroneous data. Given that work experience generally begins between 15 and 25 years of age, and considering an average retirement around 65 years of age, a reasonable range for work experience could be 0 to 50 years. Now let’s look at the people who are in a higher rank.

# Filtrar filas directamente
outliers_emp_exp <- data[data$person_emp_exp > 50, ]

# Mostrar las primeras filas
outliers_emp_exp
##       person_age person_gender person_education person_income person_emp_exp
## 82           144          male         Bachelor        300616            125
## 184          144          male        Associate        241424            121
## 576          123        female      High School         97140            101
## 748          123          male         Bachelor         94723            100
## 32298        144        female        Associate       7200766            124
## 32356         78          male        Associate         58463             57
## 32417         94          male      High School         29738             76
## 32423         80          male      High School         77894             62
## 32507         84          male      High School        114705             61
## 32535         76        female         Bachelor         90934             58
## 37931        116          male         Bachelor       5545545             93
## 38114        109          male      High School       5556399             85

## 38114              MORTGAGE      6195     VENTURE         12.58
